national_day.intro: Portugal's national day on 10 June honours poet Luís de Camões and Portuguese communities around the world.
national_day.history: 10 June marks the death in 1580 of Luís de Camões, author of the epic ‚Os Lusíadas‘. Once ‚Day of the Race‘, it was reframed under democracy as the Day of Portugal, Camões and the Portuguese Communities, celebrating language and the global diaspora.
national_day.traditions: The official ceremony moves to a different Portuguese city each year and is mirrored in a city abroad with a large Portuguese community. Military honours, concerts and parades mark the day.
funFacts: It honours poet Camões, who died on 10 June 1580.
The official ceremony changes city every year.
It celebrates Portuguese communities worldwide, not just at home.

freedom_day.intro: 25 April celebrates the 1974 Carnation Revolution that ended decades of dictatorship and restored democracy in Portugal.
freedom_day.history: On 25 April 1974 a near-bloodless military coup by the Armed Forces Movement toppled the Estado Novo dictatorship. Citizens placed red carnations in soldiers’ rifle barrels, giving the revolution its name. It led to free elections and the end of the colonial wars.
freedom_day.traditions: Lisbon’s Avenida da Liberdade fills with marches carrying red carnations, and parliament holds a solemn session. Concerts, debates and the song ‚Grândola, Vila Morena‘ recall the return of democracy.
funFacts: Red carnations in rifle barrels gave the revolution its name.
The song ‚Grândola, Vila Morena‘ was a secret signal to start the coup.
The revolution was almost entirely bloodless.
